title: The State of Prices under a Government in Disarray!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy. The printed price table was checked against source images `page_0022.png` and `page_0023.png`; clear OCR numeral, punctuation, and word-break errors have been repaired. The counterintuitive printed steel figures, Rs 48,000 and Rs 28,000, are preserved exactly. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, with the political civility of the “Jaya brand,” has been criticising the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam's manifesto for this parliamentary election as “a grand charade” and “the height of fraudulent falsehood.” Our manifesto says: “Along with taking legal measures to keep consumer prices under control, we shall take appropriate action to control the prices of essential commodities for the benefit of poor and middle-class families with limited purchasing power.” Yet the manifesto of the AIADMK, the party ruling Tamil Nadu, says nothing about controlling prices. Such is the AIADMK's concern for price control! Nearly three years have passed since Jayalalithaa took office in May 2011. How far have prices risen under this government during those years?

| Commodity | Price under DMK rule | Price under AIADMK rule | |---|---:|---:| | Toor dal, 1 kg | Rs 62 | Rs 84 | | Urad dal, 1 kg | Rs 56 | Rs 84 | | Chana dal, 1 kg | Rs 38 | Rs 74 | | Moong dal, 1 kg | Rs 80 | Rs 100 | | Garlic, 1 kg | Rs 100 | Rs 140 | | Turmeric, 1 kg | Rs 65 | Rs 160 | | New tamarind, 1 kg | Rs 100 | Rs 120 | | Old tamarind, 1 kg | Rs 60 | Rs 100 | | Chilli, 1 kg | Rs 70 | Rs 140 | | Coriander seed, 1 kg | Rs 80 | Rs 116 | | Parboiled rice, 1 kg | Rs 40 | Rs 50 | | Ponni rice, 25 kg | Rs 855 | Rs 1,315 | | Ponni raw rice, 1 kg | Rs 32 | Rs 54 | | Pepper, 1 kg | Rs 275 | Rs 680 | | Fenugreek, 1 kg | Rs 50 | Rs 100 | | Gingelly oil, 1 litre | Rs 84 | Rs 220 | | Groundnut oil, 1 litre | Rs 86 | Rs 110 | | Palm oil, 1 litre | Rs 36 | Rs 66 | | Cement, 1 bag | Rs 250 | Rs 350 | | Steel rods, 1 tonne | Rs 48,000 | Rs 28,000 | | Sand, 1 load | Rs 7,000 | Rs 19,000 | | Bricks, 1 load | Rs 13,500 | Rs 18,000 |
Under AIADMK rule, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has laboured mightily to “reduce” prices to these levels! Perhaps that is why their manifesto says nothing about controlling them.

Essential-commodity prices have climbed beyond reach, and construction-material prices too have risen. Construction has consequently suffered, leaving many thousands of workers without employment. Jayalalithaa tries to escape by blaming the Central government. If the Centre alone caused rising prices, who raised milk prices, bus fares and electricity tariffs in Tamil Nadu? If the Centre is solely responsible, why does every AIADMK Budget describe State action supposedly taken to control prices?

Almost immediately after taking office in 2011, even before presenting the Budget, Jayalalithaa departed from convention and placed roughly Rs 4,000 crore in taxes upon Tamil Nadu's people. She then burdened poor and middle-class families with higher bus fares, milk prices and electricity tariffs. While repeatedly punishing the people, she made a prodigious effort to shift blame onto the previous DMK government and the Centre.

Did the Centre not raise petrol and diesel prices while the DMK governed? It did. We strongly opposed each rise, yet the Tamil Nadu government absorbed the burden to protect the people and did not raise bus fares by even one paisa. Did we not prevent steep increases in Aavin milk prices? When the Centre raised petrol and diesel prices, did the State not forgo the corresponding sales-tax revenue?
The AIADMK raised ordinary mofussil bus fares from the DMK rate of 28 paise per kilometre by fifty per cent, another 14 paise, to 42 paise. Did it pause to think of people dependent on public buses?
It raised express and semi-deluxe mofussil fares from 32 paise per kilometre by seventy-five per cent, another 24 paise, to 56 paise. Super-deluxe and luxury mofussil fares rose from 38 to 60 paise.

Outside Chennai, the minimum town-bus fare rose by fifty per cent, from Rs 2 to Rs 3. The maximum rose by about seventy per cent, from Rs 7 to Rs 12. In Chennai, the minimum rose from Rs 2 to Rs 3 and the maximum from Rs 12 to Rs 14.

For example, the deluxe fare to Tiruchirappalli rose from Rs 105 under the DMK to Rs 180 under the AIADMK. Madurai rose from Rs 145 to Rs 255, Tirunelveli from Rs 200 to Rs 350, and Coimbatore from Rs 165 to Rs 290.

From 2006 to May 2011, the DMK government did not raise bus fares by one paisa. Jayalalithaa had raised them before 2006 and, within months of returning to office, raised them again and made the public suffer.

In every statement, Jayalalithaa accuses the DMK government and me as Chief Minister of ruining the Electricity Board. Yet when she became Chief Minister in 2001, she raised tariffs from 1 December. On 15 March 2003, while she was Chief Minister, she imposed increases totalling Rs 1,398 crore. She abolished free electricity for agriculture and huts introduced by the DMK, raised domestic tariffs by 22.13 per cent and educational-institution tariffs by 16.61 per cent.

Under the later DMK government, the Electricity Board petitioned the Regulatory Commission on 18 January 2010. After examination, revisions were announced on 31 July. There was no increase for domestic consumers using under 600 units in two months, hut dwellers, power-loom and handloom users, public places of worship or agricultural consumers.
Even domestic users above 600 units paid only one rupee more per unit. High-tension industrial consumers paid fifty paise more and low-tension users thirty paise more.

Of Tamil Nadu's 14.986 million domestic consumers, 13.467 million saw no increase under the DMK. Only 321,000 users consuming above 600 units paid one rupee more. The DMK protected those unable to bear an increase. Even that was condemned by the PMK founder, the CPI(M) and others.

Jayalalithaa herself said on 2 August 2010: “When asked to reduce prices, Chief Minister Karunanidhi acts to increase them. Homes using over 600 units in two months face higher tariffs. Monthly consumption of 300 units is common. Poor tenants will bear additional charges and rents may rise. Factories, small and micro enterprises, cottage industries and educational institutions will suffer. Essential prices may rise. The DMK government must immediately remove this burden imposed upon ordinary people.”

But upon taking office, she indiscriminately raised tariffs for every section. Within six months, her “golden” government proposed raising the domestic rate up to 50 units from 65 paise to Rs 1.50; 51 to 100 units from 75 paise to Rs 1.50; 101 to 150 units from 75 paise to Rs 2; 151 to 200 units from 85 paise to Rs 2; 201 to 500 units from Rs 2.20 to Rs 3.50; 501 to 600 units from Rs 1.80 to Rs 5.75; and above 600 units from Rs 4.05 to Rs 5.75.

Compare the DMK's protection of homes up to 600 units with the AIADMK's plan to raise prices even for those using fewer than fifty. Then decide who understands and embraces the economic condition of poor and middle-class people.

The AIADMK also proposed raising places-of-worship tariffs up to 120 units from Rs 1.50 to Rs 2.50 and above 120 units from Rs 3 to Rs 5; private educational institutions from Rs 4.80 to Rs 5; cinemas from Rs 5.50 to Rs 7; and factories from Rs 4 to Rs 5 per unit.

In a statement on 14 February 2011, Jayalalithaa accused the minority DMK government of creating a milk shortage and behaving like highway robbers instead of negotiating with producers. What happened under her rule? Without talks, she intimidated producers who sought to protest and unilaterally raised both procurement and sale prices.
The price of standardised milk for cardholders rose from Rs 17.75 to Rs 24 a litre, an increase of Rs 6.25.

Under the DMK, cow-milk procurement rose on 1 September 2009 from Rs 13.45 to Rs 15.64 and buffalo milk from Rs 18 to Rs 23. On 5 January 2011, cow milk rose to Rs 16.64 and buffalo milk to Rs 25.20. On 16 February, they rose to Rs 18 and Rs 26. Yet to protect the public, the sale price remained Rs 17.75 without even a one-paisa increase. Jayalalithaa came to power promising to lower prices and “bend the sky like a bow,” then raised milk by Rs 6.25 a litre at once.
Jayalalithaa argues that petrol and diesel increases affect prices. Does she not know that bus fares and electricity tariffs do so enormously?

The DMK government followed Perarignar Anna's counsel: “Policy decisions may slightly affect the wealthy with high purchasing power, but must never harm poor and middle-class people whose purchasing power is low.” Ignoring Anna and caring nothing for those families, Jayalalithaa dreams of passing her time by blaming others!
With affection,
M.K.
20 March 2014
